You are generating ONE complete Low-Level-Design interview session for an Uber SDE-2 prep app.

FIRST, read these two files IN FULL — they define the exact quality bar and section structure:
- /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/lld_prompt.md   (the production LLD system prompt — your §1–§9 markdown MUST match its structure, including the "## 3.5 API / System Interface" section between §3 and §4, and the §4.1 rule that EVERY class subsection ends with a COMPLETE fenced class block after its (c) operations table — never a bare table; a method-less value record still gets a fenced class block showing its fields)
- /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/agent_prompts/lld_session_builder.md   (the build recipe; IGNORE its old /Users/anshullkgarg/... paths — use the paths I give you below)

OPERATIONAL FACTS (override anything in those files):
- Workspace (create it): /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/<UUID>_lld/   with tests/ subdir.
- Run code with: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python  (NOT python3 — python3 lacks pytest here). e.g. `cd /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/<UUID>_lld && /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python main.py` and `/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python -m pytest -q`.
- Canonical flat file layout (bare names, NO packages): models.py, strategies.py (only if a Strategy is in §5), gateways.py (only if §5 names an external dep / injected Clock), <domain>_service.py (named after the DOMAIN, never service.py/utils.py), main.py, tests/conftest.py (sys.path shim), tests/test_<domain>.py.
- Python 3.11+ idioms ONLY: list[X], dict[K,V], X | None. NO 'from __future__ import annotations'. NO Optional/List/Dict. Imports at TOP of file only.
- ONE domain base exception; one specific subclass per failure mode. NEVER raise bare Exception/RuntimeError/ValueError from your own code. NEVER use a bare 'except:'; catch the narrowest correct type. If you must roll back / release a lock on ANY failure path, 'except BaseException:' then re-raise is the correct idiom.
- Money is integer CENTS — never floats for currency.
- Clean single-threaded CORE service with a no-op concurrency hook (e.g. a @contextmanager _lock() that just yields); a ThreadSafe<Domain>Service subclass overrides ONLY the hook(s). §6/§7 core is lock-free and readable; the thread-safe variant is the §9 "second version". Any slow I/O stays OUTSIDE the lock.
- NO '...'/'pass' placeholder bodies anywhere. Every method fully implemented. No dead code (every public method exercised by main.py or a test).
- Inject a Clock (gateways.py) wherever time matters so tests are deterministic.

CONCURRENCY TEST RIGOR (critical): the pytest suite MUST include an N-thread race using threading.Barrier so all threads hit the critical section at one instant, with STRICT post-state assertions — not just a count. Assert the invariant ACTUALLY bound (e.g. winners == K, len(set(winner_ids)) == K, every loser got the specific typed error, and the entity's own state/balance field confirms the conserved quantity). A single count assertion is necessary-but-NOT-sufficient: if work serialises, ADD a tiny hold (time.sleep) inside the critical section and assert the peak concurrency actually reached the limit.

MERMAID RULES (a real renderer parses these):
- Exactly TWO mermaid blocks: a ```mermaid classDiagram in §4.2 and a ```mermaid sequenceDiagram in §8.
- In sequenceDiagram MESSAGE TEXT (after the colon), NEVER use a semicolon ';' — mermaid treats ';' as a statement separator and the parse FAILS. Write "do A then B". Avoid tokens that break parsing. Keep participant aliases simple.

MARKDOWN OUTPUT:
- Write the COMPLETE §1–§9 markdown (⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan at the very top, and §3.5) to /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/<UUID>_lld/_response.md — the full artifact, NOT abbreviated. §6 code blocks tagged ```python copy and matching the disk files (no drift, no '...').
- §7.1 and §7.2 must quote the ACTUAL stdout from running main.py and pytest -q. Both must be green.

DO NOT assemble any conversation JSON and DO NOT write into /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/data/conversations/ — the orchestrator does that afterwards. Leave a WORKING workspace (tests green) + a complete _response.md.

Before finishing: run `/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python main.py` (exit 0, all cases print OK) and `/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python -m pytest -q` (fully green). If red, fix the CODE (not the test) and re-run.


=== YOUR ASSIGNED PROBLEM ===
UUID: a836a0bf-2597-4b84-9bcf-e51e582cdc15
TITLE: Design an ATM
DOMAIN (for <domain>_service.py / test_<domain>.py): atm
PROBLEM STATEMENT (put this in §1 and keep it verbatim): Design an ATM: insert card → enter PIN (authenticate) → select operation (withdraw / balance) → dispense cash and debit the account. The machine is a state machine (IDLE → CARD_INSERTED → AUTHENTICATED → DISPENSING). On any failure (wrong PIN, insufficient funds, dispense failure) roll back cleanly and eject the card. Thread-safe; the account store is shared.

DISTINCT CONCURRENCY / DATA-STRUCTURE ANGLE you MUST realize in code + §9 (this is what makes this session unique — do not drift to a generic per-resource lock):
STATE MACHINE (explicit ATM states with legal transitions) + TRANSACTIONAL withdraw with ROLLBACK ON FAILURE (debit the account and dispense as an atomic unit — if dispense fails, the debit is reversed; the account balance must never be debited without cash leaving, nor cash dispensed without a debit) + per-account lock for the shared account store. Patterns: State (primary), Strategy optional (cash-dispense denomination algorithm). Concurrency test: Barrier-sync N threads withdrawing from the SAME account that only has funds for K; assert exactly K succeed, N-K get typed InsufficientFunds, final balance == initial - K*amount (never negative), and total cash dispensed matches the total debited (no money created/lost).

Build the complete session now in /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/a836a0bf-2597-4b84-9bcf-e51e582cdc15_lld/ . Read the two prompt files first, then write code, run tests green with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python, write the full _response.md. Return the structured summary. Set has35=true only if "## 3.5 API / System Interface" is literally in _response.md, and mermaidCount to the actual count of ```mermaid blocks (must be 2).

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# LLD Session Builder — Agent Prompt
2	
3	> **Model:** `claude-opus-4-8`
4	> **How to run:** Open Claude Code CLI in your project root, switch model with `/model claude-opus-4-8`, then type the problem statement followed by the contents of this file. OR use the Agent tool from within Claude Code with `model: "opus"` and paste this entire file as the prompt.
5	
6	You are building a **complete, ready-to-open LLD (Low Level Design) interview session** for the Algora frontend. When you are done, the user must be able to open it at `https://localhost:8002/?s=<SESSION_ID>:lld` and see the full design with working code.
7	
8	---
9	
10	## What you must produce
11	
12	Given a problem statement (e.g. "Design a Parking Lot", "Design Splitwise"), you will:
13	
14	1. **Write all Python files** into the workspace directory.
15	2. **Run `main.py`** and capture the actual output.
16	3. **Write the conversation JSON** so the frontend can open the session.
17	
18	---
19	
20	## The LLD System Prompt
21	
22	Before generating anything, read the full LLD system prompt from:
23	```
24	/Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/lld_prompt.md
25	```
26	This is the exact prompt the AI uses in production. Your output must match every section it describes (§1 through §9), including the ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan at the top.
27	
28	---
29	
30	## Step 1 — Pick a session ID
31	
32	Use a fresh UUID4 for the session. The session ID format is:
33	```
34	<uuid>:lld
35	```
36	Example: `a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890:lld`
37	
38	The **slug** (used for file paths) is the session ID with every non-alphanumeric character except `-` and `_` replaced by `_`:
39	```python
40	import re
41	slug = re.sub(r'[^A-Za-z0-9_-]', '_', session_id)
42	# "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890:lld" → "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890_lld"
43	```
44	
45	---
46	
47	## Step 2 — Workspace structure
48	
49	All Python files go in:
50	```
51	/Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/workspace/<slug>/
52	```
53	
54	**CANONICAL FILE LAYOUT — use these EXACT names. Never invent `helpers.py` / `utils.py` / `service.py`.** The service file is named after the DOMAIN (e.g. `locker_service.py`, `parking_service.py`, `booking_service.py`).
55	
56	```
57	models.py             # domain entities, enums, value objects, custom exceptions (the "nouns")
58	strategies.py         # Strategy interfaces + concretes  (ONLY if §5 has a Strategy pattern)
59	gateways.py           # external-system interfaces: PaymentGateway, NotificationSink, Clock  (ONLY if §5 names one)
60	<domain>_service.py   # the orchestrator: clean single-threaded core + ThreadSafe subclass
61	main.py               # small __main__ demo driver: 3-5 cases with print(), NO test framework
62	tests/test_<domain>.py  # pytest suite: every public method, every error path, the concurrency case
63	tests/conftest.py     # adds the parent dir to sys.path so `from models import …` works in tests
64	```
65	
66	Use ~3-6 files total. Python **3.11+** idioms: `list[Foo]`, `X | None` (not `Optional[X]`), no `from __future__ import annotations`.
67	
68	### models.py must contain:
69	- All enums (`class X(IntEnum)` when ordering matters, else `class X(Enum)`)
70	- A single base exception (`<Domain>Error`) and one specific subclass per failure mode — **never mix in a bare `RuntimeError`/`Exception`/`ValueError`**
71	- All frozen dataclasses (`@dataclass(frozen=True)`) for value objects
72	- All mutable dataclasses for entities (the state-machine entity owns its own transition methods)
73	
74	### <domain>_service.py must contain:
75	- The clean single-threaded service with a `@contextmanager _lock()` no-op hook
76	- The `ThreadSafe<Domain>Service(<Domain>Service)` subclass that overrides `_lock()` with the per-key `RLock` double-lock idiom
77	- Every public method from §6, fully implemented (NO `pass`/`...` bodies)
78	- **Imports at the TOP only** — never a mid-file `import threading`
79	- **No dead code** — every method is called from `main.py` or a test
80	
81	### main.py must contain:
82	- `# Python 3.11+` as the first line
83	- A narrated demo: 3-5 cases with `print("Case N … -> OK")`, plain `assert X, message` (no `try/except False` antipattern)
84	
85	### tests/test_<domain>.py must contain (pytest-shaped):
86	- One happy-path test per public operation; one test per typed exception path; a boundary test; an idempotency test if any method is safe-to-call-twice
87	- The **concurrency test** with STRICT assertions: `len(wins) == K`, `len(set(wins)) == K` (unique reservation ids), `len({w.compartment_id …}) == K` (unique resources — catches the silent collision bug), every loss is the typed error, and the count of in-RESERVED/OCCUPIED resources == K. Use `threading.Barrier` so all N threads hit the critical section at one instant. `pytest.raises(<TypedError>)` — never bare `Exception`.
88	
89	---
90	
91	## Step 3 — Generate the full §1–§9 LLD response
92	
93	Using the system prompt from `lld_prompt.md`, generate the complete LLD response as markdown. This is what the user will READ in the frontend — it must be complete, not abbreviated. Match the CURRENT structure exactly:
94	
95	- **⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan** — table (phase | what you do | mode) + the Mode A (whiteboard, no run) vs Mode B (coding env, run) note.
96	- **§1 Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions** — 1.1 plain-words restatement (coffee-chat tone), 1.2 **candidate-LED dialogue** (`**You:**` proposes an assumption/scope-cut + asks to confirm, `**Interviewer:**` ~70% agrees, italic reaction line), 1.3 Final Requirements (numbered functional + "Out of scope" list) + 💬 opener.
97	- **§2 Actors & Core Flows** — bulleted actors + 3-5 one-line happy-path flows. No diagram here.
98	- **§3 Core Entities (accept/reject)** — 3.1 noun walkthrough accepting/**rejecting** each candidate noun with a reason, 3.2 entity table (Entity | Responsibility | Key fields typed | Invariant) + enums block, 3.3 🎙️ spoken-intro paragraph that names the rejected nouns.
99	- **§3.5 API / System Interface** — the orchestrator's **public surface only**, as ONE typed contract table (`Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose`) with 3-7 rows + ONE 🎙️ spoken line on what the surface reveals. Signatures use Python 3.11+ hints and MUST match §4.1 / §4.2 diagram / §6 code one-for-one; `Raises` lists specific typed exceptions (never bare `Exception`). Every method traces to a §1.3 requirement and vice-versa. Comes BEFORE §4 (hellointerview ordering: Requirements → Entities → API → design).
100	- **§4 Class Design** — 4.1 per-class derivation: for EVERY accepted entity (orchestrator first, then each part — entities, value objects, AND strategy interfaces) the FOUR-block format (a) one-line role → (b) state-derivation table **+ a fenced state block** → (c) operations-derivation table **+ a COMPLETE fenced class block (state + constructor + every method from the table)** → (d) design-choice Q&A. ⚠️ Every class subsection MUST end with its own complete fenced class block — never a bare (c) table. Do not let the 2nd/3rd/4th class degrade to table-only after the orchestrator got the full treatment. Then 4.2 consolidated `classDiagram` (draw-vs-say split) + relationships + Pattern Map + 🎙️ box-by-box + **⚠️ PROACTIVE CONCURRENCY FLAG**.
101	- **§5 Design Patterns & Principles** — 5.1 Information Expert first, 5.2 applied patterns (1-3, each earned, with 💬 layman gloss), 5.3 SOLID tied to named classes, 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS rule.
102	- **§5.5 Core Algorithms** — A key insight (insight + why-it-works + what-breaks-without-it), B per-operation pseudocode + STORY-driven 🎙️ scripts + a **DRY RUN** of the pipeline method with a state-change table, C data-structure choices (4 cols incl. rejected alternative), D tricky logic (🧠 Claim / 🔬 Proof / 🛠️ Fix), E ONE "❌ what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example, F narration-order table.
103	- **§6 Implementation** — **§6.0 Live Writing Order** (9 phases + 7 golden rules + TL;DR time table + mid-flight situations), then orchestrator SKELETON first, then each method in the 6-block format (role / core-logic bullets / edge-case bullets / code / reasoning PROSE / 🎙️+⚠️), ONE **Approach Comparison** for the trickiest helper, helpers written (not skipped), and a **Verification** subsection with full-lifecycle state-table dry runs.
104	- **§7 Putting It Together** — 7.1 `main.py` narrated demo output, 7.2 `tests/` pytest suite + the actual `pytest -q` output. Both green.
105	- **§8 Key Flow** — a `sequenceDiagram` of the most interesting flow + 🎙️ arrow-by-arrow narration + why-it-shows-the-design-is-good.
106	- **§9 Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility** — 4-step verbal race walkthrough, **CONCURRENCY VOCABULARY table**, **CONCURRENCY FOLLOW-UP Q&A** (why RLock, why not global, deadlock, pessimistic vs optimistic, …), the clean→thread-safe diff (Part A hook+subclass, Part B exact modified method, Part C barrier test proof), edge-cases-handled list, **Extensibility** (level-calibration note + summary table + 2-3 TWIST walkthroughs with minimal code diffs), and the hardest follow-up Q&A (concurrency/distributed/scale — distinct from the twists).
107	
108	**CRITICAL RULES FOR CODE IN §6:**
109	- NO `...` ANYWHERE. No abbreviations. No `# ... rest of implementation`.
110	- Every method body must be complete — every line, every real argument, every real error message, every real return value.
111	- Code blocks tagged ` ```python copy ` so the frontend renders a copy button.
112	- The §6 narration shows code method-by-method (teaching); the FULL files also ship as Write tool blocks (the frontend's LLD "Complete Code Implementation" tabbed viewer renders them from those `write_file` captures).
113	
114	---
115	
116	## Step 4 — Run main.py AND pytest, capture output
117	
118	```bash
119	cd /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/workspace/<slug>
120	python3 main.py
121	python3 -m pytest -q

Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions 418 This section follows the natural interview flow — first understand WHAT the system is in plain words, then ask 5-8 clarifying questions in a back-and-forth conversation with the interviewer, THEN write the final requirements. Three sub-parts: 419 420 ### 1.1 What we're building (plain words) 421 A short paragraph (3-4 sentences MAX) restating the problem in your own conversational words — not a textbook definition, not a feature list. So this section is NOT a fake Q&A where the candidate "asks" and the interviewer "answers". Instead, the candidate **PROPOSES** assumptions, scope cuts, and design decisions out loud and asks the interviewer to confirm — and 90% of the time the interviewer just agrees, because the candidate is showing they can scope the problem themselves. This is the "lead the conversation" pattern — the candidate controls the room. 426 427 Write 5-8 turns. Each turn the candidate proposes a specific assumption + the rationale + asks for confirmation. The interviewer's reply is short — usually agreement, sometimes a small adjustment, occasionally a clean pushback that the candidate accepts gracefully. Format strictly: 428 429 **You:** "[your proposed assumption / scope cut, phrased as a confident suggestion ending in a confirmation question — e.g. 'For v1 I'd assume X — that lets me skip Y. Phrasing: "I'd assume [logistics / notification delivery / payments / UI rendering / multi-region] is out of scope for this round — agreed?" Phrasing: "I'll assume codes expire after ~7 days, one code per package, 6-digit numeric. Fine for now?" 439 6. **Failure-mode contract** — propose what the system does on partial failure. Phrasing: "If the user never picks up, my plan is: code expires, sweep job reclaims the box, package marked for return-to-sender. kill the floor and the spots go with it"). 613 - **Aggregation / association** (open diamond / plain line): who holds a REFERENCE to whom, the multiplicity (1, 0..*, 1..*), and what that reference is FOR at runtime. 614 - **Dependency / uses** (dashed arrow): who transiently calls whom (e.g. the manager USES the factory) without owning it. 615 Make every multiplicity explicit, and call out the ONE relationship that is the extensibility SEAM (usually a Strategy interface the orchestrator depends on). 616 617 After all relationships, add a **Pattern Map** — a small table showing exactly which class embodies which design pattern, so the candidate can answer "which pattern is that?" instantly when the interviewer points at a box: 618 619 | Class / Interface | Pattern | One-line reason | 620 |---|---|---| 621 | [StrategyInterface] | Strategy | [OrchestratorClass] depends on the abstraction, not the concrete | 622 | [FactoryClass] | Factory Method | translates primitive API inputs into strategy objects | 623 | [ObserverInterface] | Observer | decouple notification from core booking logic | 624 | [Orchestrator]._lock hook | Template Method | no-op in clean core; thread-safe subclass overrides one method | 625 | [Repository] | Repository | separate data access from domain logic | 626 627 Fill this in for the actual classes of THIS problem — do not copy the example headers verbatim. 628 629 Then add a **🎙️ Script: walk the diagram box-by-box.** Write the exact words you'd say while pointing at each box IN THE ORDER you'd draw them: "I put [Orchestrator] in the center because every operation flows through it. Hanging off it on the left is [EntityA] — [one line on what it does]. On the right, [Interface] — this is the Strategy seam, [one line on why it's an interface]. [Concrete1] and [Concrete2] implement it — each is a one-line class. The [Observer] sits below — [one line]. The key seam is [Interface]: the orchestrator never knows which concrete it's talking to, so new [behaviors] plug in as new classes, no core changes." 630 631 ⚠️ **PROACTIVE CONCURRENCY FLAG — say this immediately after presenting the diagram, before the interviewer asks:** "I notice there's a race condition in [main operation] — if two users call it at the same time, both see 'available', both proceed, we end up double-booking. I'll keep the core design clean and single-threaded for now, and show you exactly how I'd fix that in §9." This single sentence — said proactively, with confidence, before being asked — is the highest-value signal you can give an interviewer. It shows you have built real concurrent systems. Don't wait to be asked about concurrency. Say it right here. 632 633 ## 5. Design Patterns & Principles 634 635 The goal here is NOT to list every pattern you know — it's to name the SMALL number of patterns that actually shape THIS design and explain WHY each one earned its keep. Most LLD interviews need 1-3 patterns max. Listing 6 patterns is a yellow flag — it tells the interviewer you don't know which ones are load-bearing. 636 637 ### 5.1 Information Expert (the foundational principle — always state this first) 638 Before naming any GoF pattern, state the **Information Expert** rationale that drove your class boundaries — i.e. WHO owns WHICH state, and WHY. This is the senior signal: patterns are names; Information Expert is the underlying reasoning. 639 > "I followed Information Expert: each class owns the state and behavior closest to the > data it has. [OrchestratorClass] manages allocation and the lookup map because it's > the only thing that sees all [things]. [EntityA] enforces [its-own-rule] because that > rule is intrinsic to it. [EntityB] owns its physical state because physical presence > is intrinsic to the entity, not relational to the system." 640 This single paragraph is what makes a senior candidate. Without it, the patterns below read as buzzwords. 641 642 ### 5.2 Applied patterns (1-3 of them, each one earned) 643 For EACH PATTERN (only the ones genuinely used in §6 code): 644 - **Name** the pattern 645 - **Why it fits HERE** — the SPECIFIC problem in THIS design that this pattern solves. Not "Strategy is for varying behavior" (textbook); instead "the rule for picking a compartment is exactly the thing the business will want to tweak — fragmentation today, nearest-door tomorrow — so I extract `AllocationStrategy` and the orchestrator never hardcodes the policy." 646 - **Interface + concrete impl** — one line: `AllocationStrategy.select(...) → BestFitAllocation` 647 - **🎙️ Script** — what you say out loud, conversational and confident 648 - **💬 Layman gloss** — one sentence in plain English, no jargon, the way you'd explain to a non-engineer ("It's like the locker doesn't decide which box to use — it asks a rule-of-the-day, so we can swap the rule without rewiring the locker.") 649 650 ### 5.3 SOLID principles (specific, not generic) 651 Don't list "this design respects all of SOLID" — that's empty. Pick the 2-3 principles this design SPECIFICALLY embodies and tie each to a NAMED class: 652 > - **Single Responsibility:** `Compartment` owns physical state, `AllocationStrategy` > owns selection, `LockerService` owns coordination. Three reasons to change, three > classes — never bundled. 653 > - **Open/Closed:** new policy = new `AllocationStrategy` subclass, zero core edits. 654 > - **Dependency Inversion:** `LockerService` depends on the `AllocationStrategy` > abstraction, never on `BestFitAllocation` directly. 655 656 ### 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS — the non-negotiable rule 657 This rule applies to every claim in §5.2: 658 - A pattern is "applied" ONLY if its interface/seam is visible in the §6/§7 code as a real interface with a real implementation, a real Observer registration, a real State transition. If the seam isn't in the code, it's not applied. 659 - If something is merely a future extension point, label it **"extension point (not wired in v1)"** — do NOT name-drop it under applied patterns. 660 - Do NOT claim **Singleton** unless you actually enforce a single instance (private constructor, `getInstance`, or DI scope). A class that just happens to have one instance is a **Facade**, not a Singleton — call it that. 661 - Do NOT claim **Factory** for `new Foo(...)`. A Factory is a method/class whose JOB is to translate one type into another (e.g. enum → strategy). If there's no translation, it's just construction. 662 - The interviewer WILL ask you to point at the pattern in the code. Every claim here must survive `Cmd-F`. 663 664 If after applying this rule you have ZERO patterns to claim — that's a valid answer. State it: "I didn't reach for a GoF pattern here; the design is plain Information Expert with three focused classes. If we needed to swap [behavior], I'd introduce a Strategy seam at [point]." That's a stronger answer than name-dropping an unused pattern. 665 666 ## 5.5 Core Algorithms & Approach 667 THIS IS THE MOST IMPORTANT SECTION FOR THE CANDIDATE — the bridge between the class diagram (WHAT exists) and the code (HOW it's written). Before a single line of code, the candidate must understand the algorithm and data-structure choices that drive the implementation. Cover ALL of the following: 668 669 ### A. The Key Insight (with "why it works" + "what breaks without it") 670 One sentence on the NON-OBVIOUS insight that makes this design clean — then a SHORT follow-up paragraph (3-4 sentences) explaining: - **Why it works** — what property of the problem makes this insight correct - **What would break without it** — the bad version a less-experienced engineer might write, and the specific failure that would surface in production 671 672 This three-part structure is what makes the insight teachable, not just memorable. Example shape: 673 > **Insight:** Treat each compartment as a self-guarding state machine, and make > compartment selection a swappable Strategy. 674 > 675 > **Why it works:** A compartment has a finite, ordered set of legal states > (AVAILABLE → RESERVED → OCCUPIED → AVAILABLE), and the "which compartment to use" > question is a pure policy decision with no shared state. Both naturally factor out: > the entity guards its own transitions, and the strategy returns one box. 676 > 677 > **What breaks without it:** The naive version inlines status writes inside the > orchestrator (`compartment.status = OCCUPIED` directly). Six months in, someone adds > a "RESERVED" state for partial drop-offs, but two methods forget to honor the new > transition rule — and now a compartment can be marked OCCUPIED while still RESERVED. > By making `Compartment` enforce its own transitions, that bug is impossible by > construction. 678 679 The "what breaks without it" paragraph is the senior signal — it shows you've seen the bad version in production. 680 681 ### B. Per-Operation Algorithm (pseudocode + STORY-driven script) 682 For EACH major operation, provide THREE things in order: 683 684 **(1) Pseudocode** — numbered, plain English (NOT Python). Whiteboard-shaped: every guard, every state mutation, every error path explicit. Maps 1-to-1 to §6 code so the candidate can copy line-by-line. 685 686 **(2) 🎙️ Walkthrough script** — DO NOT use the same generic "Script — say this" prefix on every operation. Each script must have its OWN character based on the method's STORY: 687 - The **pipeline method** (assign / book / order): structure as "validate → reserve → commit". Open with: "This is the pipeline — three phases." 688 - The **redemption method** (pickup / consume / use): structure as "lookup → guard → release". Open with: "Pickup is three guards and one release. The one-time-ness is in step X." 689 - The **race-prone method**: open by NAMING the race. "This method has the check-then-act race. Steps 4-6 are the critical section — I'll wrap them in §9." 690 - The **cleanup / sweep method**: open with "This runs in the background. Idempotent on purpose — each iteration is independent." 691 - The **lookup method**: open by stating the index it's hitting. "This is an O(1) hit on the `_by_code` map — that's why I built that index in `assign_package`." 692 693 The script MUST reference numbered pseudocode steps explicitly (e.g. "step 4 is where Strategy pays off"). The reader's eye should bounce between the pseudocode and the narration. Generic "this method does X then Y" is BANNED — every script must call out: 694 - The ONE non-obvious step (and WHY it's non-obvious) 695 - The step where a design pattern from §5 visibly fires (`strategy.select`, `observer.notify`, `compartment.reserve`) 696 - The step that handles the edge case the interviewer will probe ("what if the user enters the code at the exact second it expires? — step 2 catches that, look") 697 698 **(3) DRY RUN with concrete inputs** — for the SINGLE most important operation (usually the pipeline method — assign / book / charge), trace through the pseudocode with REAL values. Show the state of the key data structures BEFORE and AFTER each step. This is the strongest interview signal — it proves the algorithm with a tiny example, the way a senior engineer reviews their own code at a whiteboard. Format: 699 700 > **Dry run — `assign_package(locker_id="LKR1", package=Package(id="pkg-1", size=SMALL))`:** 701 > 702 > | Step | Action | State change | 703 > |---|---|---| 704 > | 1 | validate locker exists | locker LKR1 found ✓ | 705 > | 4 | strategy.select(locker, SMALL) | returns Compartment(C7, SMALL, AVAILABLE) | 706 > | 6 | C7.reserve() | C7.status: AVAILABLE → RESERVED | 707 > | 8 | build Reservation | code="9421", expires=now+72h | 708 > | 9 | index it | `_by_code["9421"] = res`, `_reservations[res.id] = res` | 709 > | 11 | notify ASSIGNED | observer fires with `{user, code, expires_at}` | 710 > | 12 | return res | caller receives Reservation with code "9421" | 711 > 712 > *Edge probe: what if a second agent calls `assign_package` while step 6 is mid-flight?* > *Without a lock both see C7 as AVAILABLE — that's the race I flagged. §9 fixes it with > a per-locker RLock around steps 4-9.* 713 714 Only the ONE pipeline method needs a dry run. For pickup / sweep / lookup, the pseudocode + script is enough. 715 716 **Coverage requirement:** every method that appears in the §4 class diagram and §6 code must appear in §5.5 B as pseudocode. Don't skip "boring" methods — if it's in the code, it's in §5.5. 717 718 ### C. Data Structure Choices — with rejected alternatives 719 For EVERY significant data structure, explain why THIS over WHAT-ELSE. The table needs FOUR columns, not three: 720 721 | Data Structure | Used For | Why THIS (vs alternative considered) | Complexity | 722 |---|---|---|---| 723 | `dict[id → Reservation]` | orchestrator registry | sweep needs O(1) random access by id; a list would be O(n) per cancel | O(1) get/set, O(n) sweep | 724 | `dict[code → Reservation]` | pickup by code | code is the natural key; alternative was scanning reservations on every pickup — O(n) per call → O(1) | O(1) | 725 | linear scan of compartments per locker | best-fit select | n ≤ ~50 boxes/station; per-size heap adds complexity for zero measurable win at this scale | O(n), n small | 726 727 The "vs alternative" column is what shows judgment. "dict because O(1)" is NOT enough — that's a textbook answer. "dict because scanning a list of 10K active reservations on every pickup would be O(n) and pickup is in the hot path" — THAT is a senior answer. 728 729 If you chose a SIMPLER structure over a fancier one (list scan over heap), explicitly SAY SO and give the threshold where you'd flip: "I'd switch to a per-size heap when a station crosses ~500 compartments — the scan becomes a measurable hot spot at that scale." 730 731 ### D. Tricky Logic — claim, proof, fix 732 Don't list "tricky things" as random bullets. Each entry has THREE labelled parts so the candidate can defend it under interview pressure: 733 734 **Format for each:** 735 > **🧠 Claim:** [what the tricky thing is, in one sentence] > **🔬 Proof:** [the SPECIFIC sequence of events that exposes the bug if you do it wrong] > **🛠️ Fix in code:** [the exact line / mechanism in §6 that handles it] 736 737 Example: 738 > **🧠 Claim:** `release()` must be idempotent — calling it on an already-AVAILABLE > compartment must be a no-op, not an error. 739 > 740 > **🔬 Proof:** The expiry sweep and a real pickup can race. Both decide the reservation > is done; one calls `release()` first and flips the box AVAILABLE; the second arrives > and would normally hit "illegal transition AVAILABLE → AVAILABLE" — except it'd > wrongly flag a fault on the legitimate pickup path. 741 > 742 > **🛠️ Fix in code:** `Compartment.release()` checks `if self.status in (RESERVED, > OCCUPIED): self.status = AVAILABLE` — outside that set, it returns silently. The > 50-thread test in §7/§9 exercises this exact race. 743 744 Cover 4-6 such tricky points. Race conditions, off-by-one in expiry windows, idempotency, deterministic ordering (why sort by `(size, id)` not just `size`), injected clocks, defensive copies. Each one labelled 🧠 / 🔬 / 🛠️. 745 746 ### E. The "what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example (one only) 747 Pick the SINGLE step a junior would get wrong, write the wrong version in 2-3 lines of pseudocode, and explain in one sentence WHY it's wrong. This shows you've internalised the failure mode, not just the success path. Example: 748 749 > **❌ Naive version a junior would write:** 750 > ``` 751 > def assign_package(self, locker_id, package): 752 > compartment = self.find_available(locker_id, package.size) # bare lookup 753 > compartment.status = CompartmentStatus.RESERVED # direct write 754 > return Reservation(...) 755 > ``` 756 > **Why it's wrong:** Two failure modes. (1) Direct status write bypasses the state > machine — illegal transitions become possible. (2) The find-then-write is check-then-act > — under concurrency, two callers can both see "available" and both reserve. The > correct version delegates to a Strategy, calls `compartment.reserve()` (so the entity > guards itself), and lifts the whole block under a per-locker lock in §9. 757 758 ONE anti-example for the most critical method. More than one becomes noise. 759 760 ### F. Narration Order for §6 (with method-level open lines) 761 State the exact order in which to present classes to the interviewer, and write the ONE sentence that opens each. The 🎙️ open lines in §6 should literally come from here — don't write them twice. Format: 762 763 | Order | Class | One-line open (literal — say this out loud as you start it) | 764 |---|---|---| 765 | 1 | Enums | "Let me start with the vocabulary — every state in the system, in one place." | 766 | 2 | `Compartment` (state machine) | "This is the heart — the only class allowed to change a compartment's status." | 767 | 3 | `AllocationStrategy` interface + `BestFitAllocation` | "The seam — the orchestrator never knows which policy is wired in." | 768 | 4 | `LockerService.__init__` + skeleton | "Here's the brain. Two indexes — by id for the sweep, by code for O(1) pickup." | 769 | 5 | `LockerService.assign_package` | "Now the interesting part — this is the pipeline I dry-ran above." | 770 | 6 | `LockerService.pickup_package` | "Pickup is three guards and a release. The one-time-ness is the pop on the code map." | 771 772 The open lines here become the literal `🎙️ Script` headers in §6 — never reword them. This eliminates the "every script sounds the same" problem because §5.5.F authors them ONCE, in a varied voice, and §6 just reuses them. 773 774 ## 6. Implementation — narrated, class by class 775 Write the BEST version of this code you can — production-quality, not interview-sloppy: clean and idiomatic, FULLY TYPE-HINTED (Python 3.11+ syntax: `list[Foo]`, `X | None`), small cohesive classes, precise names, a short docstring per class, **no dead code**, specific error types (every exception extends a single domain base — never bare `Exception`/`RuntimeError`/`ValueError` mixed in), and the design patterns from §5 VISIBLY wired in (a real Strategy interface with implementations, a real factory, etc.). Keep it genuinely MODULAR per using the CANONICAL FILE LAYOUT — `models.py`, `strategies.py` (if needed), `gateways.py` (if needed), `_service.py`, `main.py`, plus `tests/test_.py`. Never invent new filenames. This is the CLEAN, single-threaded-correct CORE — do NOT add any locks or thread-safety here; concurrency hardening is a deliberate SECOND version in §9. Keep §6/§7 lock-free and readable so the OO design is the star; §7's run proves FUNCTIONAL correctness only. 776 777 PRESENTATION — METHOD BY METHOD, not class by class. Inside the orchestrator, do NOT dump the whole class as one ```python block. Instead, for the orchestrator: 778 1. First show `__init__` + the method signatures (skeleton, with `...` bodies for the methods you'll fill in below). This is the API tour — interviewer sees the full surface area in one glance. 779 2. Then for EACH non-trivial method, present its OWN small ```python block followed by the 4-block format below (role / code / 🎙️ narration / ⚠️ follow-up). 780 781 The skeleton-then-flesh approach matches how the candidate would actually write the class at a whiteboard, and it makes the rendered output scannable instead of one wall of code. Small entity classes (`Compartment`, `Package`, `Reservation`, `Locker`) can still be presented as a single ```python block per file because their methods are short and tightly coupled — the per-method split is only required for the orchestrator. 782 783 **LIVE WRITING ORDER — the candidate's playbook for the live interview itself.** 784 785 This is the BIG mental-model section. The candidate cannot write the §6 code in any order they want — there's a specific phased sequence that maximises interviewer signal and minimises wasted time. ALWAYS render this as a sub-section titled "### 6.0 Live Writing Order (your interview playbook)" BEFORE any code blocks. The candidate reads this OUT LOUD pacing in their head while writing — it's their clock. Total target: ~30 minutes of code time inside a 60-minute interview. 786 787 The section has three parts in order: (a) the 9 phases with what-to-say + what-to-write, (b) the 7 golden rules, (c) a TL;DR time budget table. All three are mandatory. 788 789 #### (a) The 9 phases 790 791 Present each phase as: **Phase title + time budget** → bold "Bolo first" line (what to SAY) → then "Likho:" with a tiny representative code snippet (sub-5-line) so the candidate knows what to write at that step. The snippets are illustrative TEMPLATES adapted to THIS problem (Locker / ParkingLot / etc.) — not full re-renders of §6 code. 792 793 **Phase 1 — Set the stage (1-2 min). NO CODE YET.** 794 > Bolo first: "Main `.py`, `.py`, aur `.py` — ye 3 > files banaunga. Pehle states define karunga (enums), phir `` > ki state machine, phir orchestrator. Code likhne se pehle main aapko har class ka > role bata deta hoon." 795 > Why this matters: 30 seconds of framing tells the interviewer you're not in > chaos. Roadmap pehle, code baad mein. 796 797 **Phase 2 — Enums first (2 min). Quick win.** 798 > Bolo while writing: "States pehle — har class ki vocabulary yahaan se aati hai. > `IntEnum` use kar raha hoon size ke liye taaki 'fits' check ek line ho jaaye." 799 > Likho: 1 small ```python block with the IntEnum + 1-2 plain Enums. No methods. 800 > Why this matters: psychological — first green checkmark on the board, momentum > established. 801 802 **Phase 3 — Most-interesting entity state machine (5 min).** 803 > Bolo: "Ye system ka heart hai. Sirf yeh class apna status badal sakti hai — > orchestrator kabhi seedha status set nahi karega. Isse illegal jumps impossible > ho jaate hain by construction." 804 > Likho: the entity dataclass + every state-transition method (`reserve`, `release`, > `occupy` for Locker; `hold`, `book`, `cancel` for Seat; etc.). Idempotent guards > shown explicitly. 805 > Then immediately drop the **PROACTIVE CONCURRENCY FLAG**: "Yahan ek race > condition hogi `` mein — main usko §9 mein per-resource lock se > fix karunga. Abhi clean version likhta hoon." This single sentence is the > biggest senior signal in the whole interview. 806 807 **Phase 4 — Strategy interface + 1 concrete (1-2 min).** 808 > Bolo: "Strategy seam — orchestrator ko nahi pata best-fit hai ya nearest-door." 809 > Likho: ABC + abstractmethod + 1 concrete impl (~6 lines). 810 > If the design has no Strategy pattern, SKIP this phase entirely — don't fabricate one. 811 812 **Phase 5 — Orchestrator SKELETON (2-3 min).** ⚠️ CRITICAL. 813 > Bolo: "Pehle main saari API surface dikha deta hoon. Phir do main methods bharta hoon." 814 > Likho: ONLY `__init__` (with all fields, all injected dependencies) + EVERY public > method signature with `pass` body. Do NOT fill any body yet. 815 > Why this matters: interviewer sees the full API in one glance. If the candidate > dives straight into `assign_package`'s body, the interviewer waits anxiously > wondering what other methods exist. Skeleton answers that question once and for all. 816 817 **Phase 6 — Most important method, FULL body (5-7 min). Slow down here.** 818 > Bolo step-by-step (this is the most important 5 min of the interview). Walk > through guard clauses → critical block → notify-outside-lock → return. 819 > Likho: the full body of the orchestrator's pipeline method (`assign_package`, > `park`, `bookSeats`, `addExpense`). Include the no-op `_lock` context manager > marking the §9 boundary. Notify call OUTSIDE the lock. 820 > Narration must call out: "Notify lock ke BAAHAR hai — agar SMS slow hai to lock > mat block karo." This one sentence is a senior signal. 821 822 **Phase 7 — Second method, FULL body (3-4 min).** 823 > Bolo: "Ye redemption / completion path hai. guards, phir release." 824 > Likho: full body of `pickup_package` / `unpark` / `settle` etc. Reference §5.5.B > pseudocode 1:1 — no improvisation, just translation. 825 826 **Phase 8 — Demo verbally OR run (3-4 min).** 827 > Mode A (Amazon-style, no execution): "Main 3 cases verbally walk karunga: > happy path, capacity-full, edge case." Then SHOW state transitions in plain > text on the board: 828 > ``` 829 > assign(SMALL pkg) → C1: AVAILABLE → RESERVED, _by_code={"9421": res-1} 830 > deposit(res-1) → C1: RESERVED → OCCUPIED 831 > pickup("9421") → C1: OCCUPIED → AVAILABLE, _by_code={} 832 > ``` 833 > Mode B (Uber/Meta coding env): `python3 main.py`, show actual stdout. 834 > Wait until ALL methods are full-body before this step. A demo with one half-written > method is worse than no demo. 835 836 **Phase 9 — Concurrency (2-3 min, ONLY if asked).** 837 > Bolo first, ALWAYS: "Race `` mein hai — do callers same resource > dekh ke dono reserve kar dete hain. Fix: per-resource RLock through Template > Method — `_lock` hook ko subclass override karta hai, core code zero changes." 838 > Likho the thread-safe subclass ONLY if interviewer says "show me the code". > Otherwise stay verbal. 839 840 #### (b) The 7 golden rules — must be rendered as a numbered list 841 842 1. **Roadmap pehle bolo.** "Main 3 files banaunga, A → B → C." 30 seconds of talking saves the interviewer's anxiety and frames everything that follows. 843 844 2. **Skeleton-then-flesh.** Orchestrator gets `pass` bodies first, full bodies later. Never write the orchestrator class as one ```python blob. 845 846 3. **State machine before orchestrator.** Compartment / Seat / Spot — write these first. They're independent; orchestrator imports them. 847 848 4. **BOLO while likhing.** Silence = interviewer's brain wanders. Even a one-liner helps: "yahan clock inject kar raha hoon", "yeh guard rejection path hai". 849 850 5. **Concurrency flag drop karo entity ke baad.** Proactively, before the interviewer asks. Single biggest senior signal of the whole hour. 851 852 6. **Demo wait karo.** Even one half-written method = no demo. Show progress only once everything runs. 853 854 7. **Pseudocode → code 1:1.** §5.5.B's numbered logic becomes §6's code in the SAME order. Don't improvise live — translate the dry-run. 855 856 #### (c) TL;DR time budget table — render exactly this shape 857 858 | Time | Phase | What you're writing | 859 |---|---|---| 860 | 0-2 min | Roadmap | Just talking, no code | 861 | 2-4 min | Enums | Full enums | 862 | 4-9 min | State-machine entity | Full state machine class + methods | 863 | 9-11 min | Strategy seam | ABC + 1 concrete (skip if no Strategy in §5) | 864 | 11-14 min | Orchestrator skeleton | `__init__` + signatures with `pass` | 865 | 14-21 min | Pipeline method (`assign` / `park` / `book`) | Full body + narration | 866 | 21-25 min | Redemption method (`pickup` / `unpark` / `settle`) | Full body + narration | 867 | 25-29 min | Demo / verbal trace | 3 cases | 868 | 29-32 min | Concurrency answer | Verbal first, code only if asked | 869 870 Total: ~30 minutes of code time. In a 60-min interview, allocate 25 min for §1-§5 (talking + diagram), 30 min for §6 code, 5 min for follow-ups. 871 872 #### (d) Mid-flight situations — when things don't go to plan 873 874 Every interview hits at least one of these. The candidate must know the response before the situation arises — improvising under pressure usually makes it worse. Render this as a numbered list of 5 situations, each with a one-line response.

content.content:
875	
876	  1. **Interviewer interrupts mid-method.** STOP writing. Don't try to talk and write simultaneously — both will suffer. Address the question fully, then say "OK, where was I — I was in the middle of `<method>`, let me finish the lock block." Resume from the same line. Common mistake: keeping the cursor moving while answering → you'll write something nonsensical.
877	
878	  2. **Running out of time at the 40-min mark.** Cut in this EXACT order, never skip an earlier item to preserve a later one:
879	  > - **First cut:** second method body — describe verbally instead. "I'll describe `<second_method>` instead of writing it — same shape, three guards then release."
880	  > - **Second cut:** §6 demo / verbal trace — skip if you've narrated dry-runs in §5.5.
881	  > - **Third cut:** §9 code — stay verbal, never write the thread-safe subclass under time pressure.
882	  > - **NEVER cut:** state machine, pipeline method body, proactive concurrency flag. These three ARE the senior signal — losing any one costs more than overrunning by 2-3 min.
883	
884	  3. **Realized you need a missing field / method mid-write.** Pause for 3 seconds, voice it out loud — "actually I need a `created_at` on Reservation, let me add it" — then add it inline. 1031	## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility
1032	
1033	**DEFAULT: DESCRIBE the solution. Write code only if the interviewer explicitly asks.**
1034	
1035	In a real interview, after presenting §6 code, the interviewer will probe: "What about concurrent users?" Your answer should be verbal first — clear, confident, specific. Only write the thread-safe version if they say "show me the code" or "implement it."
1036	
1037	**DESCRIBE THIS VERBALLY (always):**
1038	
1039	🗣️ **Step 1 — Name the exact race condition:**
1040	"The problem is in [method name]. Two users call it at the same time. Both check — both see 'available'. Both proceed. Both succeed. We have double-booking. This is called a check-then-act problem — the check and the action are not one atomic step."
1041	
1042	🗣️ **Step 2 — State the solution:**
1043	"I'd add a lock to the [OrchestratorClass]. Before [method] starts checking, it acquires the lock. While it holds the lock, no other thread can enter. After the check-and-assign completes, it releases the lock. Now both users race to acquire the lock — only one wins, the other waits, then sees the resource is already taken."
1044	
1045	🗣️ **Step 3 — Name the trade-off:**
1046	"The coarse lock is simple and correct. The downside: only one [operation] can happen at a time. If we need higher throughput, we'd move to per-resource locking — each [resource] has its own lock, so ten different users booking ten different [resources] don't block each other."
1047	
1048	🗣️ **Step 4 — Address the distributed case (if asked):**
1049	"For multiple servers, an in-process lock is not enough — each server has its own memory, so my Python lock only protects ONE process. We'd push the atomicity down to the database with an optimistic lock — 'mark this resource as held, but ONLY if it's still available' — one SQL write that checks and updates in a single atomic step: `UPDATE compartment SET status='RESERVED' WHERE id=? AND status='AVAILABLE'`. Whoever's update affects 0 rows lost the race and retries or gets a clean error."
1050	
1051	**CONCURRENCY VOCABULARY — be fluent in these (the candidate must be able to define each in plain words AND point to where it appears in THIS design).** An interviewer scores concurrency on whether you USE the right words correctly, not just whether the code works. Provide a compact table mapping each term to a plain definition + where it bites in this specific problem:
1052	
1053	| Term | Plain meaning | Where it appears here |
1054	|---|---|---|
1055	| **Race condition** | Two threads touch shared state at once and the result depends on timing | `select` + `reserve` in `assign_package` |
1056	| **Critical section** | The block that must run as one atomic step | the select-reserve-index block |
1057	| **Check-then-act** | You check a condition, then act on it — but state changed in between | "saw AVAILABLE, then reserved" — the classic bug |
1058	| **Atomic** | All-or-nothing — no other thread can observe a half-done state | what the lock guarantees for the critical section |
1059	| **Mutex / lock** | A token only one thread holds at a time | `threading.Lock` / `RLock` on the locker |
1060	| **Reentrant (RLock)** | The SAME thread can re-acquire a lock it already holds | `pickup` holds the lock and calls `_expire`, which re-takes it |
1061	| **Lock granularity** | How much one lock covers — coarse (one global) vs fine (per-resource) | we chose per-LOCKER, not one global lock |
1062	| **Pessimistic locking** | Grab the lock BEFORE touching state (assume conflict) | the in-process `RLock` approach |
1063	| **Optimistic locking** | Don't lock; write conditionally and retry if you lost (assume no conflict) | the DB compare-and-set for the distributed case |
1064	| **Compare-and-set (CAS)** | Atomic "update only if value is still X" | the `WHERE status='AVAILABLE'` SQL clause |
1065	| **Deadlock** | Two threads each hold a lock the other needs — both wait forever | avoided here: we only ever hold ONE lock at a time |
1066	| **Starvation** | A thread never gets the lock because others keep winning | not a risk here; locks are short-held |
1067	
1068	The candidate reads this and can DROP these terms naturally: "this is a check-then-act race in the critical section; I take a pessimistic per-locker mutex — reentrant because `pickup` re-enters via `_expire` — and for the distributed case I switch to optimistic locking with a compare-and-set." That single sentence signals senior-level fluency.

1070	**CONCURRENCY FOLLOW-UP Q&A — the probing questions after your basic answer.** Interviewers rarely stop at "add a lock." They probe. Give the EXACT spoken answer to each — 4-6 of these, chosen for THIS problem:
1071	
1072	> ❓ "Why `RLock` and not a plain `Lock`?" > "Because a method that holds the lock calls another method that ALSO takes the lock — > `pickup` holds it and calls `_expire`, which re-acquires it. A plain `Lock` would > deadlock on the second acquire by the same thread; an `RLock` (reentrant lock) lets > the owning thread re-enter, and only fully releases when the outermost `with` exits."
1073	
1074	> ❓ "Why not just lock the whole method, or use one global lock?" > "One global lock is correct but kills throughput — every station serializes through > one bottleneck even though they share no state. Per-LOCKER locking lets ten different > stations book in parallel; only agents at the SAME station serialize, and that's > microseconds. Locking the whole method also needlessly holds the lock during the > notify call — I keep `notify` OUTSIDE the lock so a slow SMS never blocks other agents."
1075	
1076	> ❓ "What's the deadlock risk?" > "Deadlock needs two locks acquired in different orders. Here I only ever hold ONE lock > at a time — the per-locker lock — and the notify/IO happens outside it. 1077	
1078	> ❓ "Pessimistic or optimistic — which did you pick and why?" > "Pessimistic in-process because the critical section is tiny and contention at one > station is low — grabbing a short-held lock is simpler and has no retry loop. For the > DISTRIBUTED case I flip to optimistic: a DB compare-and-set, because you can't hold a > Python lock across servers, and at that layer a conditional write + retry is the > idiomatic atomic primitive."
1079	
1080	> ❓ "This is read-heavy (lots of pickups, few deposits) — would a read-write lock help?" > "If reads dominated and didn't mutate, a read-write lock would let many readers proceed > in parallel and only block on writes. But here even `pickup` MUTATES (it frees the box > and burns the code), so it's not a pure read — a read-write lock wouldn't buy much. > I'd reach for it only if I had a genuinely read-only query path."
1081	
1082	> ❓ "What if the lock-holder crashes mid-critical-section?" > "In-process, a crash takes the whole process down, so the half-done state is gone with > it — there's no orphaned lock to clean up. # Python 3.11+
"""Domain model for the ATM: enums, value objects, the Account entity, and the
typed exception hierarchy.

The ATM problem is really two intertwined ideas:
  1. The machine is a STATE MACHINE — it moves IDLE -> CARD_INSERTED ->
     AUTHENTICATED -> DISPENSING and back, and illegal jumps must be impossible.
  2. A withdrawal is a TRANSACTION — debit the account AND dispense cash as one
     all-or-nothing unit. If the cash drawer cannot pay out, the debit is
     reversed. Money must never leave the account without cash leaving the
     machine, and cash must never leave the machine without a matching debit.

This file holds the "nouns": the states, the Account (the shared, mutable
money holder), and every typed error. Nothing here knows about threads.
"""
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum


# ── WHY THIS ENUM ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# The ATM's life is a small, fixed set of states. Naming them as an enum (not
# bare strings) means the type checker and our transition guard both see the
# exact legal vocabulary — a typo like "AUTHENTICTED" fails loudly.
# 🎙️ "The machine is a state machine. These four states are its entire life:
#     idle, card-in, authenticated, dispensing. Every operation is a guarded
#     move between two of these."
class ATMState(Enum):
    IDLE = "IDLE"                    # no card in the slot — the resting state
    CARD_INSERTED = "CARD_INSERTED"  # card read, PIN not yet verified
    AUTHENTICATED = "AUTHENTICATED"  # PIN verified — operations allowed
    DISPENSING = "DISPENSING"        # mid-withdrawal — debiting and paying out


class OperationType(Enum):
    WITHDRAW = "WITHDRAW"
    BALANCE = "BALANCE"


# ── WHY THIS BASE EXCEPTION ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
# One base per domain. Every failure the ATM can produce is a subclass, so a
# caller can `except ATMError` to catch anything we throw, or catch a specific
# subclass to handle one failure mode. We NEVER raise bare ValueError/Exception
# from our own code — that would force callers to catch the whole world.
class ATMError(Exception):
    """Base class for every error this ATM can raise."""


class IllegalStateError(ATMError):
    """An operation was attempted from a state where it is not legal
    (e.g. entering a PIN with no card inserted)."""


class AuthenticationError(ATMError):
    """The PIN did not match the card's account."""


class InsufficientFundsError(ATMError):
    """The account balance cannot cover the requested withdrawal."""


class DispenseError(ATMError):
    """The cash drawer physically could not dispense the requested amount
    (e.g. out of cash / out of notes). Raised by the dispenser; the service
    catches it to roll the debit back."""


class UnknownAccountError(ATMError):
    """The card points at an account the bank store does not know."""


class InvalidAmountError(ATMError):
    """A withdrawal amount was non-positive or not a whole-currency amount."""


# ── WHY THIS VALUE OBJECT ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# A Card is just the link from the physical plastic to an account plus the PIN
# the bank expects. It never changes once issued, so it is frozen — Python
# stops any accidental mutation. The PIN lives on the card here only to keep the
# demo self-contained; in production the bank verifies the PIN server-side.
# 🎙️ "Card is a frozen value object — plastic that points at an account and
#     carries the expected PIN. It has no behaviour, it just identifies."
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Card:
    card_id: str       # the id printed on the plastic
    account_id: str    # which account this card draws from
    pin: str           # the PIN the bank expects for this card


# ── WHY THIS ENTITY ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# The Account is the SHARED, MUTABLE money holder — the one thing multiple ATMs
# (and threads) touch at once. It owns its balance and the two transition
# methods that change it: debit and credit. Money is stored as integer CENTS,
# never a float, so we never get 0.1 + 0.2 == 0.30000000000000004 rounding bugs.
# 🎙️ "Account is the shared state. It holds cents as an int and guards its own
#     balance: debit refuses to go negative, credit is how we reverse a debit on
#     a failed dispense. The orchestrator never writes balance directly."
@dataclass
class Account:
    account_id: str
    balance_cents: int

    def debit(self, amount_cents: int) -> None:
        # WHY: the account guards its own invariant — balance can never go
        #      negative. The service checks funds before calling, but the
        #      account double-guards so the rule is true BY CONSTRUCTION even if
        #      a future caller forgets the pre-check.
        if amount_cents <= 0:
            raise InvalidAmountError(f"debit amount must be positive, got {amount_cents}")

        if amount_cents > self.balance_cents:
            raise InsufficientFundsError(
                f"account {self.account_id} balance {self.balance_cents} "
                f"cannot cover debit {amount_cents}"
            )
        self.balance_cents -= amount_cents

    def credit(self, amount_cents: int) -> None:
        # WHY: credit is the REVERSE of debit. We use it for exactly one thing —
        #      undoing a debit when the cash drawer fails to dispense. Keeping it
        #      tiny and obvious makes the rollback path easy to read and trust.
        if amount_cents <= 0:
            raise InvalidAmountError(f"credit amount must be positive, got {amount_cents}")
        self.balance_cents += amount_cents


# ── WHY THIS VALUE OBJECT ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# The receipt is the proof the transaction completed — how much left the account
# and the exact notes that left the drawer. It is frozen because a completed
# transaction must never change after the fact. The `notes` field is what lets a
# test assert "cash dispensed == amount debited" note-for-note.
# 🎙️ "WithdrawalResult is the receipt — frozen, because a finished withdrawal is
#     history. It carries the cents debited and the exact notes dispensed, so the
#     conservation-of-money check is just comparing two numbers."
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class WithdrawalResult:
    account_id: str
    amount_cents: int
    notes: dict[int, int] = field(default_factory=dict)  # note value (cents) -> count

    @property
    def dispensed_cents(self) -> int:
        # WHY: derive the cash total from the notes so it can NEVER drift from
        #      the amount field — they are computed from the same source of truth.
        return sum(value * count for value, count in self.notes.items())

# Python 3.11+
"""The ATM orchestrator.

`ATM` is the brain. It owns the current state, the card in the slot, and a
reference to the shared AccountStore. It enforces TWO things:

  1. The STATE MACHINE — every public call is only legal from certain states.
     `_transition` is the single chokepoint that validates and applies a move,
     so an illegal jump (e.g. withdraw before authenticate) is impossible.

  2. The TRANSACTION — `withdraw` debits the account and dispenses cash as one
     all-or-nothing unit. If the dispenser raises, we credit the money straight
     back and re-raise, so the balance is exactly what it was before.

Concurrency: the SHARED thing is the AccountStore — many ATMs draw on the same
account. The clean `ATM` below is single-threaded and lock-free for readability.
`ThreadSafeATM` is the §9 second version: it overrides ONE hook (`_account_lock`)
to take a per-account lock around the debit-and-dispense critical section.
"""
import itertools
import threading
from collections.abc import Iterator
from contextlib import contextmanager

from gateways import CashDispenser, Clock
from models import (
    Account,
    ATMState,
    AuthenticationError,
    Card,
    DispenseError,
    IllegalStateError,
    InsufficientFundsError,
    InvalidAmountError,
    OperationType,
    UnknownAccountError,
    WithdrawalResult,
)


# ── WHY THIS CLASS ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# The AccountStore is the shared bank store — the single source of truth for
# balances that every ATM reads and writes. It is its own class (not a bare
# dict on the ATM) because it is SHARED across ATMs: one store, many machines.
# That sharing is exactly what makes the per-account lock necessary in §9.
# 🎙️ "The account store is shared by every ATM. It's the thing two machines can
#     race on, so it gets its own class and, in the thread-safe version, its own
#     per-account locks."
class AccountStore:
    def __init__(self, accounts: list[Account]) -> None:
        self._accounts: dict[str, Account] = {a.account_id: a for a in accounts}

    def get(self, account_id: str) -> Account:
        account = self._accounts.get(account_id)

        if account is None:
            raise UnknownAccountError(f"no account {account_id}")
        return account


# ── WHY THIS CLASS ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# The ATM is the system's public API and its state machine. External code calls
# insert_card -> enter_pin -> withdraw/check_balance -> eject_card, and every one
# of those calls is a guarded transition. Keeping the legal-moves map in ONE
# place (`_LEGAL`) means the rules are data, not scattered if-statements.
class ATM:
    """A single ATM terminal. Single-threaded, lock-free core."""

    # WHY: the legal transitions are DATA, not code. One table the whole class
    #      reads from. Adding a state later means editing this map, not hunting
    #      through methods for status writes.
    _LEGAL: dict[ATMState, set[ATMState]] = {
        ATMState.IDLE: {ATMState.CARD_INSERTED},
        ATMState.CARD_INSERTED: {ATMState.AUTHENTICATED, ATMState.IDLE},
        ATMState.AUTHENTICATED: {ATMState.DISPENSING, ATMState.IDLE},
        ATMState.DISPENSING: {ATMState.AUTHENTICATED, ATMState.IDLE},
    }

    def __init__(self, store: AccountStore, dispenser: CashDispenser, clock: Clock) -> None:
        self._store = store
        self._dispenser = dispenser
        self._clock = clock
        self._state: ATMState = ATMState.IDLE
        self._card: Card | None = None          # the card currently in the slot
        self._counter = itertools.count(1)      # numbered tx ids for readable logs

    @property
    def state(self) -> ATMState:
        return self._state

    # ── the state-machine chokepoint ────────────────────────────────────────
    def _transition(self, target: ATMState) -> None:
        # WHY: the ONLY place self._state is ever written. It checks the move is
        #      in the legal-moves table before applying it, so an illegal jump
        #      (withdraw straight from IDLE) raises instead of corrupting state.
        if target not in self._LEGAL[self._state]:
            raise IllegalStateError(
                f"cannot go {self._state.value} -> {target.value}"
            )
        self._state = target

    def _require(self, *states: ATMState) -> None:
        # WHY: a tiny guard the public methods call first — "you must be in one
        #      of these states to do this". Keeps each method's body short.
        if self._state not in states:
            allowed = " or ".join(s.value for s in states)
            raise IllegalStateError(
                f"operation needs state {allowed}, but ATM is {self._state.value}"
            )

    # ── no-op concurrency hook (Template Method) ─────────────────────────────
    @contextmanager
    def _account_lock(self, account_id: str) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: does NOTHING in the clean core — there are zero locks in this file.
        #      The thread-safe subclass overrides ONLY this method with a real
        #      per-account lock. The diff between clean and safe is this one hook.
        yield

    # ── public API: the card / PIN / operation flow ─────────────────────────
    def insert_card(self, card: Card) -> None:
        """Insert a card. IDLE -> CARD_INSERTED."""
        self._require(ATMState.IDLE)
        # WHY: validate the card points at a known account BEFORE accepting it.
        #      We fail at the boundary rather than discovering it at withdraw time.
        self._store.get(card.account_id)
        self._card = card
        self._transition(ATMState.CARD_INSERTED)

    def enter_pin(self, pin: str) -> None:
        """Authenticate. CARD_INSERTED -> AUTHENTICATED on match; on a wrong PIN
        we roll back cleanly and eject the card."""
        self._require(ATMState.CARD_INSERTED)
        card = self._active_card()

        if pin != card.pin:
            # WHY: wrong PIN is a failure path — roll back to IDLE and eject so
            #      the next user starts clean. We raise AFTER ejecting so the card
            #      never gets stuck in the machine on a bad PIN.
            self._reset()
            raise AuthenticationError("incorrect PIN — card ejected")
        self._transition(ATMState.AUTHENTICATED)

    def check_balance(self) -> int:
        """Return the account balance in cents. Needs AUTHENTICATED."""
        self._require(ATMState.AUTHENTICATED)
        account = self._store.get(self._active_card().account_id)
        return account.balance_cents

    def withdraw(self, amount_cents: int) -> WithdrawalResult:
        """The transactional withdrawal. Needs AUTHENTICATED.

        Debit and dispense are one all-or-nothing unit: debit first, then ask
        the drawer to pay out. If the drawer cannot, we credit the money back so
        the balance never ends up debited without cash leaving the machine."""
        self._require(ATMState.AUTHENTICATED)
        self._validate_amount(amount_cents)
        account = self._store.get(self._active_card().account_id)

        self._transition(ATMState.DISPENSING)
        # WHY: the whole debit+dispense runs inside the account lock so two ATMs
        #      drawing on the SAME account cannot both pass the funds check. In
        #      the clean core the lock is a no-op; the thread-safe subclass makes
        #      it real. This block is the critical section.
        with self._account_lock(account.account_id):
            result = self._debit_then_dispense(account, amount_cents)

        # WHY: back to AUTHENTICATED so the user can do another operation or
        #      eject. We leave DISPENSING only after the money question is fully
        #      resolved — committed or rolled back.
        self._transition(ATMState.AUTHENTICATED)
        return result

    def eject_card(self) -> None:
        """End the session from any state. Always returns the machine to IDLE."""
        # WHY: eject must work from ANY non-idle state — a user can hit cancel
        #      mid-flow. It is the universal clean exit. Idempotent-ish: ejecting
        #      with no card is just a no-op reset.
        self._reset()

    # ── private helpers ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    def _debit_then_dispense(self, account: Account, amount_cents: int) -> WithdrawalResult:
        # WHY: this is the heart of the transaction. Debit the account first.
        #      Then try to dispense. If the drawer raises, we MUST give the money
        #      back — `except BaseException` then re-raise is the correct rollback
        #      idiom: undo our partial change on ANY failure, then let the error
        #      propagate so the caller still sees what went wrong.
        account.debit(amount_cents)
        try:
            notes = self._dispenser.dispense(amount_cents)
        except BaseException:
            account.credit(amount_cents)   # roll the debit back — money restored
            raise
        next(self._counter)  # advance the tx id (readable audit numbering)
        return WithdrawalResult(account.account_id, amount_cents, notes)

    def _validate_amount(self, amount_cents: int) -> None:
        # WHY: reject bad amounts at the boundary with a typed error, before we
        #      touch any state. Withdrawals must be a positive whole-cent value.
        if amount_cents <= 0:
            raise InvalidAmountError(f"withdrawal amount must be positive, got {amount_cents}")

    def _active_card(self) -> Card:
        # WHY: any operation that needs a card calls this. If the state machine
        #      is correct there is always a card here, but we guard anyway so a
        #      logic bug surfaces as a typed error, not an AttributeError on None.
        if self._card is None:
            raise IllegalStateError("no card in the machine")
        return self._card

    def _reset(self) -> None:
        # WHY: the single clean-exit path — drop the card and return to IDLE.
        #      Used by eject and by every failure path (wrong PIN, etc.) so the
        #      machine never gets stuck in a half-open state.
        self._card = None
        self._state = ATMState.IDLE

    @staticmethod
    def supported_operations() -> tuple[OperationType, ...]:
        # WHY: surfaces the operation menu. Used by main.py to show the user what
        #      the authenticated machine can do — keeps the menu in one place.
        return (OperationType.WITHDRAW, OperationType.BALANCE)


# ── WHY THIS SUBCLASS ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# This is the §9 "second version". It changes NOTHING about the state machine or
# the transaction logic — it overrides only the `_account_lock` hook so the
# debit-and-dispense critical section runs under a real per-account lock.
# 🎙️ "The thread-safe version is a subclass that overrides one method. Per
#     account, not one global lock, so two people on two different accounts never
#     wait on each other — only people on the SAME account serialize."
class ThreadSafeATM(ATM):
    def __init__(self, store: AccountStore, dispenser: CashDispenser, clock: Clock) -> None:
        super().__init__(store, dispenser, clock)
        self._map_lock = threading.Lock()                 # guards the dict of locks
        self._locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {}      # account_id -> its lock

    @contextmanager
    def _account_lock(self, account_id: str) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: lock the dict of locks FIRST, then grab (or create) this account's
        #      lock. Without the map lock, two threads creating the same account's
        #      lock at once could each make a different lock object and both
        #      "win". RLock so a future guarded helper calling another guarded
        #      helper on the same thread does not deadlock on itself.
        with self._map_lock:
            lock = self._locks.setdefault(account_id, threading.RLock())
        with lock:
            yield
